claims.text: The Dulce, New Mexico area allegedly hosts the highest per-capita UFO sighting rate in the world and has been the subject of decades of rumors about a hidden joint human-alien underground base inside the Archuleta Mesa.

evidence.quote:
the area
has the largest number of UFO sightings
per capita in the world

newer stories claim there's an
underground base within the Archuleta
Mesa near Dulce although there's never
been hard evidence of this base

claims.rationale: The 'highest UFO sightings per capita' claim is regularly repeated in UFO literature about Dulce but has no rigorous demographic or sighting-database backing — 'sightings per capita' is a heuristic invented by enthusiasts. The Dulce-base story itself is one of the best-known modern conspiracy narratives (Bishop, Bennewitz, Schneider, Castello, Lear) but no credible physical, photographic, or document evidence of an underground base inside Archuleta Mesa has surfaced. Low directTruth on the underlying base-existence claim.

claims.text: In 1975 Officer Gabe Valdez investigated cattle mutilations near Dulce — animals fully drained of blood, organs surgically removed, with tripod scorch marks, a yellow oily residue, and (later) elevated radiation in the area — establishing the original anomalous evidence around the Dulce story.

evidence.quote:
in 1975 a rancher in Dulce
called the police in a panic one of his
cattle had been killed at first he
thought it was a predator but predators
don't do this officer Gabe Valdez
responded to the call he couldn't
believe what he found the cow was
drained of all its blood and had
undergone a procedure where many of its
organs were surgically removed and
around the cow's carcass were tracks in
the soil that looked like they came from
the feet of a tripod next to each of the
tracks the ground was scorched

over the
following months 17 animals would be
killed this way all were missing their
lymph nodes all or missing multiple
organs and all taken surgically all the
animals were drained of blood and the
little blood that remained wouldn't clot
not even after several days soon
investigators started to become sick

claims.rationale: Cattle mutilations in northern New Mexico (Rio Arriba County, Dulce area) in the mid-1970s are real and well-documented — Officer Gabe Valdez of the New Mexico State Police did investigate them, and Senator Harrison Schmitt convened a 1979 conference on the subject. The FBI's 1980 'Operation Animal Mutilation' (the Rommel Report) and later journalism (Linda Moulton Howe, Charles Strozier) confirm the case histories. However, the alleged 'tripod scorch marks' and 'unidentifiable yellow oil' details, plus the supernatural framing, are contested — many of the cases are now attributable to predator scavenging plus rapid decomposition.

claims.text: Despite the apparently anomalous case file, the FBI formally closed its mutilation investigation by declaring it 'consistent with predator action' — leaving private investigators to fill the explanatory vacuum with alien hypotheses.

evidence.quote:
the FBI said quote it was
consistent with predator action and
there is no federal interest in
continuing an investigation the only
Predator that can do this is the
Predator

claims.rationale: The FBI did formally close its investigation of cattle mutilations in 1980, and the Rommel Report concluded that most cases were consistent with predator and scavenger activity post-mortem. The host's framing that this 'left a vacuum' is editorial — many investigators including Valdez himself contested the FBI's conclusion. The factual claim about the FBI's closing language is essentially correct.

claims.text: Albuquerque physicist-engineer Paul Bennewitz, who lived next to Kirtland Air Force Base — the Western Hemisphere's largest nuclear-weapons storage site — observed and reported strange lights and radio transmissions near the Manzano weapon-storage complex, drawing him into a years-long entanglement with U.S. Air Force counter-intelligence.

evidence.quote:
Paul Bennett was born in 1927 and when
World War II broke out he joined the
Coast Guard as a teenager he had a
master's degree in physics and was
pursuing his PhD when he opened an
electronics company in Albuquerque New
Mexico Paul benewitz's primary clients
were the Air Force in NASA he did so
much business with the air force that he
actually bought a home and established a
lab right next to Kirtland Air Force
Base

Kirtland also happens to be the
largest storage facility for nuclear
weapons in the Western Hemisphere

claims.rationale: Paul Bennewitz (born 1927) was a real Albuquerque-based scientist/engineer with Air Force contracts who did own a home next to Kirtland AFB and did report extensive UFO observations and intercepted 'alien' radio signals — many of which were later acknowledged as Air Force radar test signals (KC-135 / radar-cross-section work). The Manzano nuclear weapons complex is real. Greg Bishop's 'Project Beta' (2005) is the definitive history of the Bennewitz case. Host's facts here are essentially correct.

claims.text: The Myrna Hansen abduction case (1980) — recovered under hypnosis with the help of Bennewitz and Valdez — produced the foundational claim that abductees were taken to an underground base where alien-human genetic experiments were conducted, complete with an alleged surgical implant near the base of the skull.

evidence.quote:
Myrna Hansen claimed to have had a
terrifying experience

Myrna was
taken to the nearby Lovelace Medical
Center and X-rayed at the base of her
skull right where she said it would be
was the implant

claims.rationale: Myrna Hansen's 1980 hypnosis sessions (conducted by psychologist Leo Sprinkle, organized through Bennewitz and Valdez) are documented and form the seed of the Dulce-base abduction lore. The 'implant' was reported, but no rigorous independent medical analysis has confirmed it as artificial. Hypnosis-recovered abduction memories are widely considered unreliable in the psychology literature (Loftus, French, Spanos). The case is real history; the supernatural conclusions are not.

claims.text: In 1995, contractor-engineer Phil Schneider went public at the Preparedness Expo claiming he had been wounded in 1979 in a 'Battle of Dulce' against gray aliens that killed 66 American soldiers — the cornerstone first-person account of an alien-military firefight at Dulce base.

evidence.quote:
in 1995 at
the preparedness Expo Phil Schneider

in 1979
Schneider was working for Morrison
Knutson on a geology team digging
exploratory shafts for a new underground
base in Dulce and the working with heavy
duty equipment the drills kept breaking

66 men
lost their lives in what was called the
Battle of Dulce base

claims.rationale: Phil Schneider really did give numerous lectures in 1995-96 (the 'Preparedness Expo' was a real circuit). However, his story has multiple verifiable holes (see c_0010): his employment timeline at Morrison-Knudsen, his characterization of NATO/Delta Force/Secret Service involvement (anachronistic and procedurally impossible), the missing-finger-from-college-electrical-line account from his roommate, the fabricated U-boat/Naval-officer father biography. There is no independent corroboration of '66 dead soldiers' in any military or news record. Low directTruth.

claims.text: Schneider's broader claims — 131 U.S. underground bases linked by Mach-2 maglev trains, four U.S.-alien treaties (1944/1954/1962/1979), the 'Greada Treaty' authorizing alien abductions in exchange for technology, and even alien-supplied mini-nukes used in the 1993 WTC and 1995 Oklahoma City bombings — illustrate how internally consistent yet evidence-free a fully developed conspiracy mythos can become.

evidence.quote:
as of
1995 there were 131 active secret
underground bases

four treaties with the aliens in
1944 in 1954 1962 and the last one in
1979 after the Dolce battle the treaty
signed in 1954 called the griata treaty
was signed between the U.S

the bombings of the
World Trade Center in 1993 and the
Oklahoma City bombing of 1995 were done
with miniature nuclear devices provided
by the aliens

claims.rationale: These are all extreme claims with zero credible supporting evidence. The 1993 WTC bombing is a documented FBI investigation (Yousef et al., truck bomb of urea nitrate); the 1995 Oklahoma City bombing was a fertilizer/diesel ANFO truck bomb (McVeigh/Nichols); both are well-investigated and have nothing to do with mini-nukes. The 'Greada Treaty' originates with William Cooper (Behold a Pale Horse, 1991) and has no documentary basis. Maglev tunnel networks at Mach 2 connecting 131 bases is physically and economically implausible. directTruth essentially zero.

claims.text: Phil Schneider was found dead in his apartment in January 1996, four months after his Preparedness Expo talk; his death was officially ruled suicide-by-strangulation, but missing physical evidence, a refusing medical examiner, and his prior statements ('if I'm found dead it's murder') have made his death central to the lore.

evidence.quote:
in January 1996 just four
months after he gave his famous lecture
Phil Schneider was found dead in his
apartment

claims.rationale: Phil Schneider was indeed found dead in his Wilsonville, Oregon apartment on January 17, 1996. The death was officially ruled a suicide by asphyxiation (a rubber catheter tied around his neck). His friends, family, and ex-wife Cynthia Drayer have publicly maintained it was murder, citing several of the irregularities the host lists. There is genuine ambiguity about manner of death, though the medical examiner's official ruling stands. Moderately high directTruth on the basic facts; the 'murder' interpretation is contested.

claims.text: A second alleged Dulce insider, Thomas Castello — supposedly a security photographer at the base who turned whistleblower in 1987 — provided the iconic seven-level taxonomy (security, human housing, executive, mind control, alien housing, genetic experiments / 'the zoo', cryogenics) that defines the Dulce base mythology.

evidence.quote:
Thomas Castello was a military
photographer with top secret clearance

level one is security and
Communications level two is human staff
and housing wait a minute if there's a
flaw labeled human staff then that means
there is housing for non-human staff no
level three is Executive offices and
Labs level four is mind control
experiments level five is alien housing
level six is genetic experiments and the
zoo

claims.rationale: Thomas Castello / Costello is widely regarded by UFO researchers (e.g., Adam Gorightly, Greg Bishop) as a fictional construct invented by either Anne West or Branton (Tau Levesque). The host explicitly says later that 'no record of anyone named Thomas Castello was found in the U.S military during the time he was supposed to be there.' The seven-level Dulce schematic is the most-circulated piece of UFO conspiracy literature but has zero corroboration. Near-zero directTruth.

claims.text: Pilot John Lear — son of Lear Jet inventor William Lear, ex-CIA cargo pilot, and 17-time aviation record-holder — became one of the most prominent post-Bennewitz amplifiers of the Dulce / hostile-alien thesis with his 1988 'UFO Cover-Up' document.

evidence.quote:
Lear became interested in
UFOs in 1986

in 1988 Lear released a
document titled UFO cover-up

claims.rationale: John Lear (1942-2022) really was William Lear's son, a longtime aviator with FAA records of multiple speed/altitude class records, and an intelligence-adjacent contract pilot (he flew for various CIA-affiliated airlines). His 1988 'Statement Released by John Lear' (sometimes called the 'Lear Document' or 'UFO Cover-Up') circulated widely and asserted exactly the things the video summarizes. Background biography ~true; the underlying alien-conspiracy claims are unverified.

claims.text: Phil Schneider's account contains specific anachronisms and falsehoods — Delta Force was barely 18 months old in 1979, NATO has never operated on U.S. soil, Black Berets weren't standard at the time, and his father's documented Navy biography contradicts his Nazi U-Boat-captain story — strongly suggesting his testimony was confabulation rather than insider disclosure.

evidence.quote: obviously there's no hard

Phil Schneider had a documented
history of mental illness

claims.rationale: All four debunkings are accurate: Delta Force was created in late 1977 — about 18 months prior to the alleged 1979 incident — and was secret and tiny; NATO is a treaty alliance with no domestic U.S. operational mandate; Army Rangers wear tan/khaki berets, with black berets historically associated with armored cavalry pre-2001 and standard Army adoption from 2001; Oscar Schneider's documented U.S. Navy medical-corps service (with credentials confirmed in the Naval Medical Corps history) contradicts the 'U-Boat captain' claim. The host's takedown is strong and well-supported by independent debunkers (Rene Barnett, James Black).

claims.text: The Dulce-base story can be partially traced to a single architect — pseudonymous UFO writer 'Branton' (Tau Levesque) — whose 1970s Hollow Earth newsletter recycled 1947 Shaver-mystery pulp fiction; subsequent researchers each layered their own embellishments onto a foundation that wasn't journalism but creative writing.

evidence.quote:
if you trace back the history of the
Dulce base story

the book saucers Spooks and Kooks UFO
disinformation in the age of Aquarius
written by Adam go rightly identifies
Levesque as the Chief Architect

claims.rationale: Adam Gorightly's 'Saucers, Spooks and Kooks' (2021) is a real, well-sourced book on UFO disinformation that does identify 'Branton' (real name disputed; Levesque is one identification) as a primary author/elaborator of the Dulce mythos. The Shaver Mystery (Richard Shaver, Amazing Stories, 1945-49) is a documented science-fiction precursor to many underground-alien tropes. The historical-genealogy reasoning is solid.

claims.text: It is documented that Air Force OSI agent Richard Doty actively fed disinformation to Paul Bennewitz and the wider UFO community — meaning the Dulce mythos was, at minimum, partly the artifact of a real government counter-intelligence operation rather than disclosure.

evidence.quote:
Richard
Doty ran a misinformation campaign
against benowitz and the entire UFO
Community feeding them wild stories
about aliens

claims.rationale: Richard C. Doty's role as an AFOSI counter-intelligence officer who fed false documents (notably the 'Project Aquarius' material and the so-called MJ-12 papers) to Bennewitz, Linda Moulton Howe, William Moore, and others is now openly admitted by Doty himself in the documentary 'Mirage Men' (2013), and corroborated by Bill Moore's 1989 MUFON speech and Greg Bishop's 'Project Beta'. This is one of the best-documented counter-intelligence ops in UFO history.

claims.text: AJ's verdict: he wants the UFO field to be more rigorous, but in this specific case the evidence base is so contaminated by mental illness, government disinformation, and pulp-fiction borrowing that the Dulce-base story almost certainly isn't true — even though the broader idea of secret U.S. underground facilities is plausible.

evidence.quote:
abductions genetic
experiments organ harvesting and a new
world order we better hope Phil

claims.rationale: Pure host opinion / verdict, framed as 'I want to believe but this isn't the one'. Not a falsifiable empirical claim, so directTruth is omitted. Captures AJ's signature 'skeptical believer' stance.
